article_title: Microsoft Open-Sources 86-DOS 1.00 on Its 45th Anniversary, Releasing Code Transcribed From Tim Paterson's Original Printouts
summary: Submission approvable with 1 minor warning(s) — file a corrections record on merge

content_preview.title: Microsoft Open-Sources 86-DOS 1.00 on Its 45th Anniversary, Releasing Code Transcribed From Tim Paterson's Original Printouts
content_preview.summary: On April 28, 2026, Microsoft published the earliest known DOS source code under the MIT License, including the 86-DOS 1.00 kernel and PC-DOS 1.00 development snapshots transcribed from Tim Paterson's printouts.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Microsoft has released what it describes as the earliest known DOS source code as open source, publishing the 86-DOS 1.00 kernel along with development snapshots of the PC-DOS 1.00 kernel and utilities such as CHKDSK. The materials were posted to a GitHub repository named [DOS-History/Paterson-Listings](https://github.com/DOS-History/Paterson-Listings) on April 28, 2026, the 45th anniversary of 86-DOS 1.00, [according to the Microsoft Open Source Blog](https://opensource.microsoft.c...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).
Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News piece (671 words) with clear Overview / What We Know / What We Don't Know / Why It Matters sections. Tone is neutral and appropriately professional throughout — no editorializing, no AI self-reference. The author explicitly handles a sourced disagreement (the $75K vs ~$100K acquisition price) instead of papering over it, which is good editorial practice.
editor_notes.source_verification:
Read all 6 gzipped snapshots from disk (source-0 through source-5).

• source-0.html.gz (Microsoft Open Source Blog): Confirms blog post 'Continuing the story of early DOS development', published 2026-04-28, authored by Stacey Haffner (Director, Microsoft OSPO) and Scott Hanselman (VP, Member of Technical Staff, Microsoft/GitHub). Confirms team 'led by Yufeng Gao and Rich Cini', the prior open-sourcings (MS-DOS 1.25/2.11 in 2018, MS-DOS 4.0 in 2024), and the verbatim phrase 'licensed under MIT via pull request'.

• source-1.html.gz (Tom's Hardware, Mark Tyson, 2026-05-02): Confirms the verbatim quote 'is perfect and recompiles byte for byte to the original binaries' attributed to a Microsoft VP, and the 'found in a garage' detail with 'reams of old dot matrix printouts'.

• source-2.html.gz (It's FOSS, Sourav Rudra, 2026-04-30): Confirms the verbatim quote 'Tim did not hand over a tidy source archive; instead, what he kept were physical assembler printouts and stacks of continuous-feed paper from 1981' and the verbatim phrase 'locate, scan, and transcribe the DOS-related portions into compilable code'. Confirms '$100,000' acquisition figure and 'August 1981' shipment to IBM as PC DOS 1.0.

• source-3.html.gz (PCWorld): Confirms competing '$75,000' acquisition figure and 'PC-DOS 1.0 was ready in August 1981'. The article correctly flags the $75K vs ~$100K discrepancy as unresolved.

• source-4.html.gz (GitHub repo DOS-History/Paterson-Listings): Confirms repository description 'Transcription of Tim Paterson's DOS printouts', the three top-level directories (1_transcription, 2_printed_files, 3_source_code), the verbatim contents description '86-DOS 1.00 kernel, various PC-DOS 1.00 pre-release kernels and utilities, and the Microsoft BASIC-86 Compiler runtime library', and 'Seattle Computer Products' ASM assembler' targeting. README states 'MIT license'.

• source-5.html.gz (Open Source For You, Apurba Sen, 2026-05-04): Confirms the verbatim phrase 'support study, preservation, and exploration of computing history' and the included materials list (86-DOS 1.00 kernel, PC-DOS 1.00 development snapshots, CHKDSK, assembler listings).

Every inline link in the body matches a source that contains the specific claim attached to it. No misattribution detected.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All factual claims verified against snapshots. The article's careful handling of the $75K vs $100K discrepancy — explicitly stating 'the two figures cannot both be exact, and neither outlet documents an underlying contract reference' — is exactly the kind of bounded reporting that strengthens credibility. No hallucinated quotes; the 4+ direct quotations all appear verbatim in the cited snapshots.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral, factual, slightly contemplative in the closing 'Why It Matters' section without crossing into editorializing. No sensationalism, no loaded language.
editor_notes.originality: New topic — searched articles archive for '86-dos', 'paterson', 'dos source' and found no prior coverage. The closest adjacent article is the 2024 MS-DOS 4.0 release referenced inside this article's body, which is appropriate context, not duplication.
recommendations: Consider adding opensource.microsoft.com (Microsoft's official open source blog), itsfoss.com, and pcworld.com to the source allowlist as a future housekeeping task — all three are well-established, reputable outlets that have appeared in multiple Machine Herald submissions.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality News submission. All claims sourced and verified verbatim against the snapshots. The verdict is APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS solely because 5 of the 6 source domains are not in the allowlist (Tom's Hardware is the only one that is) — a technical compliance issue rather than a content issue. The corrections file documents this gap. The article itself is publishable as-is.
